w06.title: Total Cost of Ownership and Environmental Impact
w06.topic: Total cost of ownership and environmental impact of light bulbs.
w06.lo1: Model the total cost of ownership (TCO) of different bulb types as a function of usage hours.
w06.lo2: Perform a sensitivity analysis on electricity tariff and usage intensity.
w06.lo3: Quantify lifecycle CO₂ emissions and discuss the embodied carbon of bulb manufacturing.
w06.resources: Bulb specification table; calculator or spreadsheet software (recommended); access to current Dutch electricity tariff data.
w06.intro: When choosing between light bulbs, the cheapest option depends on how long you use them. For a lamp that is on for only a few minutes per day, an LED may never pay back its higher purchase price. For a lamp that burns 12 hours a day, an LED is far cheaper within a year. In this worksheet you will build a Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) model and analyse when each technology is the rational choice.

prereq.label: What you should already know:
prereq.body: This worksheet assumes you have completed Worksheet 1B (or are confident with luminous efficacy, kWh calculations, and the simple TCO concept).

theory.title: Theory
theory.intro: At this level we treat bulb selection as an optimisation problem. The TCO function from Worksheet 1B can be written more rigorously and analysed mathematically.
theory.formulaIntro: The continuous form of TCO over a usage period of t hours is:
theory.formulaWhere: where L is bulb lifetime (h), Pbulb is the unit purchase price (€), W is the wattage and τ is the electricity tariff (€/kWh). Strictly speaking, you cannot buy 0.7 of a bulb, so the realistic version uses the ceiling function ⌈t/L⌉ for the number of bulbs needed.

be.label: Break-even analysis:
be.body: comparing two bulb types A and B, the break-even hours t* is the value of t where TCOA(t) = TCOB(t). Below t*, the cheaper-to-buy option wins; above t*, the more efficient option wins. Solving for t* algebraically gives insight into when switching technology actually pays off.

sens.label: Sensitivity:
sens.body: real-world conclusions depend on input assumptions (especially τ, which has varied between €0.10 and €0.70 per kWh in the Netherlands in recent years). A robust analysis tests how the conclusion changes when key inputs change.

life.label: Lifecycle CO₂:
life.body: the total environmental impact includes both manufacturing emissions (embodied carbon) and emissions from electricity used during the bulb’s lifetime. Using the 2024 Dutch grid factor of 0.27 kg CO₂/kWh, manufacturing typically dominates LED lifecycle emissions whereas electricity use dominates incandescent lifecycle emissions.

worked.title: Worked example — break-even between incandescent and LED
worked.setup: Find the break-even hours between an incandescent bulb (W = 60, P = €0.50, L = 1,000 h) and an LED (W = 12, P = €1.33, L = 25,000 h) at τ = €0.30/kWh.
worked.s3: Set equal: 0.0185·t = 0.003653·t → this gives 0 = −0.014847·t, so they are equal only at t = 0.
worked.s4: Conclusion: with these prices the LED is cheaper from hour 1 onwards (its TCO grows much more slowly). The "break-even" question only becomes interesting if the LED’s purchase price is much higher (e.g. €15), in which case there is a real positive break-even point.

act1.intro: The total cost of ownership over a period of t hours can be written as:
act1.where: where L is the lifetime of one bulb (h), Pbulb is the purchase price (€), W is the wattage (W), τ is the electricity tariff (€/kWh), and ⌈·⌉ is the ceiling function (round up to whole bulbs).
act1.q1: Derive a simplified version of this formula by assuming continuous bulb replacement (i.e. ignore the ceiling function). Show your derivation.
act1.q2: Plot (or sketch) TCO(t) for all four bulb types on the same axes for t between 0 and 25,000 hours. Use τ = €0.30/kWh.
act1.q3: Calculate the break-even point in hours between (a) incandescent vs. LED and (b) CFL vs. LED. Below which usage does the older technology still “win” on TCO?

act2.intro: Real-world electricity tariffs in the Netherlands have varied between €0.10/kWh and €0.70/kWh in the last five years.
act2.q1: Recompute the LED-vs-incandescent break-even point for τ = €0.10 and τ = €0.70. How sensitive is the conclusion to electricity price?
act2.q2: A user only uses a particular lamp 30 minutes per day. Over the 25,000-hour LED lifetime, how many calendar years is that? Is the LED still the rational choice here?

act3.intro: 1 kWh of Dutch grid electricity emits approximately 0.27 kg of CO₂ (2024 average; was 0.46 kg in 2018). Manufacturing one LED bulb is estimated to emit ~1.5 kg CO₂; one incandescent bulb ~0.3 kg.
act3.q1: Calculate the total lifecycle CO₂ emissions (manufacturing + use) for 25,000 hours of light, for both LED and incandescent. Use the 2024 grid factor.
act3.q2: What share of total LED lifecycle emissions comes from manufacturing? What does this imply about the value of replacing working incandescent bulbs versus waiting until they fail?
